seasonal_wine.name: Winter pruning across the vineyard, January to February
seasonal_wine.description: Deep winter is the pruning season. Across the Bas-Rhin and Haut-Rhin growers walk the rows trimming canes through the cold, the quiet work that sets each vine's crop for the coming vintage. Cellar tastings are unhurried and appointments easy to land.
seasonal_wine.tip: January and February are the easiest months to land a private domaine appointment; ask growers about cane versus spur pruning in their Riesling parcels.

seasonal_wine.name: Budbreak and frost watch, April
seasonal_wine.description: Budbreak arrives in April and with it the threat of spring frost across the lower-elevation parcels. Growers light candles and run wind machines through cold dawns, a fight that has cut yields in recent vintages from 2017 onward.
what_to_taste: Sylvaner and Pinot Blanc as fresh aperitifs
seasonal_wine.tip: April is shoulder season with light visitor traffic; ask growers to walk you through their frost-protection set-up, a vivid window into how fragile the start of the vintage can be.

seasonal_wine.name: Slow Up Alsace cycling Sunday, early June
seasonal_wine.description: On the first Sunday of June, 35 kilometres of the wine route between Bergheim and Marlenheim close to traffic for Slow Up Alsace. Tens of thousands of cyclists and walkers cross the vineyards, with growers and villages offering tastings and food stops along the way.
what_to_taste: Crisp Pinot Blanc poured by roadside growers
Cool Sylvaner on a bike-stop terrace
seasonal_wine.tip: Hire bikes in Bergheim or Marlenheim and ride mid-morning to beat the crowds; the route links several Grand Cru villages so plan tasting stops by appellation.

seasonal_wine.name: Flowering and fruit set, June
seasonal_wine.description: Flowering through June fixes the size of the crop, with roughly a hundred days from flowering to harvest as a rule of thumb. Warm settled weather points to a generous vintage; rain and coulure thin the bunches and concentrate flavour.
what_to_taste: Chilled Pinot Blanc among the vines
Light Pinot Noir as an early-summer red
seasonal_wine.tip: Count roughly a hundred days from flowering to estimate harvest; a mid-June flowering usually means a late-September start to picking under classic conditions.

seasonal_wine.name: Foires aux vins and summer festival season, July to August
seasonal_wine.description: High summer is the season of the foires aux vins. From the Foire aux Vins de Ribeauville in July to the headline Foire aux Vins d'Alsace in Colmar across late July and August, village after village pours its growers' wines under marquees and along old streets.
what_to_taste: Cremant d'Alsace at the Colmar fair
seasonal_wine.tip: Buy the festival tasting glass at the gate (usually a few euros) and use it across the run; the Colmar fair doubles as a major concert festival, so book hotels months ahead.

seasonal_wine.name: Veraison and pre-harvest, late August
seasonal_wine.description: Late August brings veraison, when Pinot Noir colours up and Gewurztraminer and Pinot Gris soften. Growers taste through the rows checking sugar and acidity, and recent warm vintages have pushed first picks into late August, especially for Cremant base wine.
what_to_taste: Estate barrel samples of the prior vintage
seasonal_wine.tip: Late August is the calm before harvest; ask whether the estate green-harvests, a telling sign of how it manages ripeness in warm vintages.

seasonal_wine.name: Vendange and Cremant base picking, September
seasonal_wine.description: Harvest sweeps Alsace through September, Cremant base fruit and Pinot Noir first, then Riesling and Gewurztraminer through the month. Recent warm vintages have moved the start earlier; villages buzz with pickers, tractors and the smell of fermenting must.
what_to_taste: Cellar-side fermenting must on private visits
seasonal_wine.tip: Confirm harvest dates with each estate before travelling, they move year to year; midweek visits avoid weekend tourist crowds and give the best chance of seeing pickers in the rows.

seasonal_wine.name: Sentiers gourmands gourmet walks, September to October
seasonal_wine.description: Across autumn weekends, villages from Bennwihr and Mittelbergheim to Eguisheim and Kaysersberg run their sentiers gourmands: timed walks through the vines with successive courses and matched glasses of local wine at each stop.
what_to_taste: Pinot Noir with the main course
Vendanges Tardives Pinot Gris with dessert
seasonal_wine.tip: Book tickets weeks ahead, especially for the Mittelbergheim and Eguisheim editions; bring proper walking shoes since the routes climb and descend through real vineyard tracks.

seasonal_wine.name: Vendanges Tardives and Selection de Grains Nobles, October to November
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, October and November in suitable vintages
seasonal_wine.description: In suitable years growers leave parcels of Riesling, Gewurztraminer, Pinot Gris and Muscat on the vine into late October or November for Vendanges Tardives, and rarer Selection de Grains Nobles with noble rot. The 2001 decree sets the legal minima.
what_to_taste: Late-harvest Riesling with a cool acidity
seasonal_wine.tip: Not every year produces Vendanges Tardives; ask before travelling if recent vintages have delivered the late-harvest cuvees, and pair them with foie gras or roquefort.

seasonal_wine.name: Noel d'Alsace and Christmas wine cellars, December
seasonal_wine.description: Strasbourg's Christkindelsmarik runs from late November to Christmas Eve and draws a couple of million visitors. Across the wine route, growers from Eguisheim to Bergheim open Christmas cellars pouring vin chaud and Cremant under the village lights.
what_to_taste: Cremant d'Alsace as a Christmas aperitif
Hot mulled Pinot Noir at the markets
seasonal_wine.tip: Strasbourg books out for the markets; for a quieter alternative head to Eguisheim, Riquewihr or Kaysersberg, all of which run their own decorated Christmas-cellar trails along the wine route.
